data.title: Brookfield Infrastructure: Boring But Strong Case For Retirement Income
data.description:
I have been covering Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIP.UN:CA)(BIPC) since early 2024 and issuing quarterly earnings updates on a rather regular basis.

All of my articles have been bullish, as BIP's current income value proposition has remained strong. By the "current income value prop," I mean the following aspects:

  • Material dividend yield.
  • Inflation-linked cash flows.
  • Potential for gradual price appreciation even though the income is clearly the primary return driver.
  • Resilient business model.

All in all, I think that BIP is a security, which simply has to be included in retirement income-seeking investor portfolios. As stated above, the offered yield is decent enough to move the needle, and the fundamentals are defensive enough to provide the necessary safety for retirees and, in general, prudent income investors.

Now, my previous piece on BIP was circulated when the Q1, 2025 report was released. The stock is up by 11%, and the total return figure is 14%.

At the same time, the yield remains enticing - at 5.5%. The P/FFO does not indicate any overvaluation. In fact, I would say that the P/FFO of 9.5x signals that BIP is in a bargain territory.

Let me discuss the Q2, 2025 earnings report and update the thesis accordingly.

Thesis review

Before I jump into the details, let's take a quick look at the chart below:

This chart illustrates how BIP has managed to increase the FFO per share in a very consistent and attractive fashion. This period includes such situations as the oil shock, COVID-19, fears of China slowing down, rapidly rising interest rates etc. Despite all of these ups and downs, the FFO per share figure has ticked up without experiencing any hiccups in the process.

And it is quite important to note that the FFO is expressed here on a per share (or unit) basis, which means that the ~8% CAGR has not been fueled by dilutive equity sales.

If we turn to the Q2, 2025 result, we will simply see a clear continuation of this trend. Namely, in Q2, BIP generated FFO per share of $0.81, which translates to 5% year-over-year growth. Adjusted for the FX swings, the FFO per share is up by 9%. It is totally fine that we see FX-driven fluctuations, which in some quarters introduce positive effects and in some (like this time) negative adjustments. The key is that the underlying business performance remains robust and shows no sign of stopping.

The FFO increase was mostly driven by the combination of the following factors:

  • Embedded CPI escalators.
  • Organic growth.
  • CapEx and M&A-driven growth.

Let me just provide two examples, which capture the business case nicely.

First, the midstream segment generated FFO of $157 million, which is 10% above the FFO result achieved in the same quarter last year. The midstream segment is one of the largest for BIP and is inherently durable with predictable cash flow generation.

Second, the data segment generated $113 million, which is 45% above the figure registered in the same quarter last year. And this is a segment, where BIP has heavily concentrated its CapEx agenda. It will require just two or three years for the data segment to become the largest FFO contributor for BIP. So, effectively, BIP is moving in a direction of having assets that carry relatively rich valuation multiples. Given the aforementioned single digit P/FFO, I think that it would be fair to assume that sooner or later we will be talking about price-return potential more than the dividend case.

Speaking of cash distributions, the 3-year CAGR profile is clearly above the inflation rate (i.e., at ~6%, which goes nicely together with the realized FFO growth). In fact, even though the distribution increases have been material, since the rate of change in the FFO figure has surpassed the dividend hikes, we can now enjoy a growth-accommodative FFO payout of 68%.

Finally, in the Q2 data points we can notice another aspect, which is supportive of sustainable growth. And by sustainable growth I mean CapEx and M&A strategies that are not facilitated at the expense of BIP's investment-grade profile. One thing is the FFO retention that stems from a conservative payout, but it is even more important to factor in the benefits of selected infrastructure project monetizations.

On a YTD basis, BIP has secured $2.4 billion of fresh liquidity through accretive asset sales. To illustrate how all of this is accretive:

  • In Q2, BIP secured a sale of a 23% interest in its Australian export terminal (metallurgical coal export facility), from which BIP is set to collect $280 million at a cumulative return of 22% and a multiple of initially invested capital of 4x.
  • In the same quarter, BIP entered into an agreement to divest partial stakes of its U.K. port operations, from which it will capture about $385 million at an IRR of 19% and a 7.5x multiple of invested capital.

The bottom line

The Q2 earnings data confirm yet again that BIP is a solid retirement income play. The underlying cash flows have kept growing and in a manner that even exceeds the distribution hikes. On top of that, we see a heavier tilt towards the data segment, which is what eventually will provide us with a justified base for expecting a positive valuation reset. Finally, it is also encouraging to see that the growth gets funded by very enticing project sales, which validate the case that BIP should indeed trade at a higher multiple than ~P/FFO 9.5x and, importantly, that the growth does not come at the expense of prudent financial profile.

With this, I am keeping the buy rating unchanged.

data.title: Enphase: I Was Wrong, Updating My Bullish Thesis
data.description:
I have been wrong—very wrong—on Enphase Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:ENPH), as the stock proved far more cyclical than expected. It is anyone’s guess what 2026 might bring as the company is faced with the end of solar tax credits. In the meantime, the company remains profitable on a GAAP basis with a net cash balance sheet, but I expect pressure on both items over the next year or more. The stock still looks buyable here given the high torque to lower interest rates or a change in the political climate. I reiterate my buy rating but stress the peculiarities of the bullish thesis.

ENPH Stock Price

I last covered ENPH in May, where I stuck by my buy rating but noted the increasing risk amidst the planned end of solar tax credits. The stock has underperformed the broader market by around 10% since then.

There is much uncertainty ahead, but the company is well-positioned financially and competitively to potentially emerge with a stronger market share.

ENPH Stock Key Metrics

ENPH is a leader in residential solar with a focus on microinverters. This technology is often viewed as offering superior reliability relative to string inverters, which previously helped the company perform strongly against competitor SolarEdge Technologies, Inc. (SEDG). However, the company is still facing stiff competition from Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), which ironically does employ string inverters. The company also faces great headwinds from the Trump Tax Bill, which would phase out 30% residential solar credits by next year. While the tax credit guidance indicates less stringent requirements than previously feared, there undoubtedly would be a drop-off in demand that is likely to persist over the coming years.

That last point creates a difficult overhang for the stock that may create the risk of the stock being “dead money” for a while, as investors are likely to desire less uncertainty. The company recently generated solid top and bottom line growth (exceeding the high end of revenue guidance) while remaining profitable on a GAAP basis, but it is highly unclear how the company might fare next year and beyond.

ENPH ended the quarter with $1.5 billion of cash versus $1.2 billion of debt, representing a net cash balance sheet. I wouldn’t be surprised to see the balance sheet get tested in 2026 as demand wanes. The company repurchased around $30 million of stock in the quarter, but I expect share repurchases to end soon due to the uncertain drop-off in demand despite the low stock price.

Looking ahead, management guided for the third quarter to see around $350 million in revenue, which would represent a return to YoY declines.

On the conference call, management noted that their “microinverter channel inventory was slightly elevated.” Management expects that inventory will be corrected by the end of the year due to an anticipated pull-forward in demand by consumers ahead of the drop-off of the solar tax credits. Management noted that they expect tariffs to impact gross margins by around 4% as “there is no safe haven.” Management expects this to reduce over time as they improve battery cost discipline, but tariffs appear to be yet another headwind facing the company. Management believes that their total addressable market might experience a “20% drop” next year due to the end of the solar tax credits. Management intends to react to the changing landscape by improving their leasing partnerships as well as concentrating on strong execution. My interpretation is that the company is facing factors beyond its control, and investors need to just wait for the coming quarters to see if the fear is overblown.

Is ENPH Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

It can be difficult to understand ENPH as it is effectively trading in “no man’s land.” The stock recently traded hands at just around 3x sales. Consensus estimates do not appear to have factored in the impact of the end of tax credits—I wouldn’t be surprised if 2026 saw revenues decline in the double-digit range.

The stock was trading at around 14x consensus earnings estimates, but those are based on 23.8% non-GAAP net margins. The company’s GAAP net margin hovered at around 10% in the most recent quarter (placing the stock at around 24x GAAP earnings), and I again stress that earnings might take a hit next year.

We can try to estimate the projected impact if the company sees revenues decline by 20%, in line with management’s projection for the decline in TAM. Assuming gross margins remain steady at 47%, I project a 20% decline in the top-line to lead to as much as a 92% decline in GAAP operating income. The company would curiously still remain profitable on a GAAP basis. I note that SolarEdge Technologies, Inc. (SEDG) saw its gross margins decline to the negative area over the last several years, and ENPH would see an even larger impact if it experienced the same fate.

Yet why am I still sticking by my buy rating for the stock? For starters, I would be surprised if ENPH really sees such a terrible hit to its business as described above. But the more important point is that between lower interest rates and future administrations to reinstate the solar tax credit, ENPH appears highly exposed to cyclical upswings in the future. The stock trades at reasonable valuations based on current fundamentals and may offer significant upside if the stars align. I also expect the company to benefit starting in 2027 when it starts lapping the easier comparables, though I acknowledge that 2025 was supposed to be the year in which it was lapping the easier comparables from rising competition from TSLA. There does indeed appear to be a moving goalpost problem here, as the company is continually presented with challenges ranging from interest rates, competition, and tariffs to the removal of tax credits. I stress that I find the stock buyable, but reiterate that my past bullishness was quite misguided, especially in terms of conviction. The thesis for ENPH is, unfortunately, now for the company to survive the next tough year, without much hope for aggressive share repurchases or anything like that, given the likely strain on cash flows. It is possible that the company can take market share as the weaker operators struggle, but this stock needs one of the two catalysts (or both) above in order to really embark on a sustainable move higher.

To quantify the potential upside, I can see the stock trading up to around 35x earnings, which, based on 20% net margins, would imply a 7x sales multiple. That represents tremendous upside from current levels, but the catalyst to return to sustainable growth remains elusive.

ENPH Stock Risks

There are great risks here. I cannot understate the reliance on external factors driving the bullish thesis. Unless I am mistaken, GAAP profitability is about to be substantially pressured (though it might somehow remain positive). Without meaningful GAAP profits, the company might not be able to take advantage of these low stock prices, as the low valuation is predicated on the company eventually achieving higher margins in the future. It is possible that the company sees margins pressured more than expected, leading to pressure on the balance sheet. It is possible that competition from TSLA proves worse than expected, and ENPH becomes the hunted in the tough macro conditions.

ENPH Stock Conclusion

ENPH is one of the messier stories that I cover, as I typically focus on secular growth compounders. While ENPH is still GAAP profitable with a solid balance sheet, I caution that both of these metrics may come under heavy fire over the next year as the tax credit rolls off. I nonetheless expect the company to survive through this extended bearish winter, as the potential upside remains considerable if and when conditions change. The next couple of quarters are likely to show great volatility as consensus estimates still appear too optimistic regarding 2026 estimates and beyond. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock but again point to the increased detail regarding conviction and external factors beyond the company’s control.

data.title: Shell: The Refocusing Effort Continues
data.description:
Shell plc (NYSE:SHEL) began a transformative move from the Netherlands to the United Kingdom (London) a couple of years ago. This decision occurred after a certain court case proved to be "a bridge too far" for company management and came after years of the dual shares to avoid taxation issues. Now with the move comes an effort to refocus the company on profits as opposed to political correctness.

The latest announcement was the abandonment of the Rotterdam biofuels project, which will cause yet another impairment charge. This may turn out to be a "clear the decks" year as the company refocuses on profits by streamlining operations and dumping "losers." There will be renewable energy in the future of the company. But from now on, it will "pull its own weight."

Earnings

Shell already has some impairment charges in the current fiscal year. It should be noted that many of these are write-offs of money previously spent. Therefore, a focus on the cash flow statement is probably a priority. In the accounting world, money already spent is "water over the dam" in that there is no current effect. What impairments do is reduce the value of the asset to its net realizable value (if any).

It can be easily seen from the report that the company has been rationalizing operations in the various divisions. The most significant change is the "Renewables and Energy Solutions", which saw most of the attributed loss going to "Identified Items Before Taxation." Note that in fiscal year 2024, there was a gain.

But as the comparison above demonstrates, there has clearly been a move to dump some laggards, sell some others, and in general increase the profitability of the company. The good news appears to be that, despite the recent announcement, the value of these "adjustments" is lagging behind the value of the previous fiscal year. That may indicate that the "housecleaning" is getting near completion.

The announcement today that Shell will not restart construction on the Rotterdam project basically means that this project will be written down to a residual value in the third quarter, if that has not happened already. But as noted before, sunk costs do not reflect the current cash flow situation. Instead, they reflect past ideas that really did not work out for one reason or another.

Cash Flow

Both adjusted earnings and cash flow are down from the previous fiscal year (probably due to weakening commodity prices). But that makes a refocusing effort more possible, according to many managements. Here, the whole thing appears to have begun in the previous fiscal year.

Much of the industry's earnings peaked in fiscal year 2022 when commodity prices went sky-high. On one hand, the refocusing effort should increase corporate profitability going forward. On the other hand, any time this happens, investors have to question how the company got off track and whether that issue was resolved.

Clearly the cash flow appears to be in good shape compared to net debt. Now the company does appear to have a fairly sizable cash balance. Whether that balance will come down as a result of the streamlining is anyone's guess. If it did, then the debt the cash balance is offsetting would be paid to reduce interest expense.

Note that starting with adjusted earnings is not a bad idea, as that is clearly the most relatable figure to current cash flow.

The long-term goal has to be to avoid spending money that results in impairment charges because that money was spent on an idea that essentially failed. Too much of that can affect the finances of the company materially.

So far, it would appear that the company will escape from the venture into renewables with impairment charges that current earnings will replace rather quickly. It has often been stated that the best managements do a lot more right than wrong in terms of significance. Investors will have to see what comes next to determine if what is going on now was the proper strategy.

Stock Price

The company has not been around all that long in its present form. However, Shell, the business, is a very old and storied business.

What is interesting is that this is one of the few companies in the oil and gas industry that has had a rising stock price since fiscal year 2022 despite the decline of commodity prices from robust heights in that same year.

That stock price action could indicate that the company is on the right path.

Last Article

The last article noted that Shell was reporting strong results back in November of fiscal year 2024. The total return is comparable to what the S&P benchmark has done during the same time period despite weakening commodity prices.

A performance of the stock like what is shown above can be an indication of very strong management. A whole lot of companies I follow wish that they had the recent stock price action shown above because many stocks have headed in the same direction as the weakening commodity prices.

Summary

For me, Shell remains an excellent growth and income idea. My suspicion is that this management is taking full advantage of the relocation to improve the company's profitability substantially. Even taking that into account, the price-earnings ratio is on the cheap side for this market, and the dividend yield is more than reasonable for a growth and income idea.

Shell is a very large company. The diversification and size of the company generally reduce investment risk to make the lower return of a large company's growth and income idea acceptable.

I would consider holding until a material change in the strategy or until the stock price far exceeds its value. But in a market that may be about to enter into a cyclical downturn, this could turn out to be a very positive investment that will fully participate in any coming recovery.

Risks

There is always the risk that the company gets "off track" again and decides to head into a bunch of losing businesses. That would result in yet another round of refocusing and write-offs similar to what is being seen right now.

The market tends to value cyclical companies based on average profitability throughout the business cycle. Too many instances of write-offs affect the average profitability of the company and hence its valuation. But this is why cyclical companies "tend" to look expensive at cyclical bottoms while being cheap at cyclical highs.

There will be renewables and other forays into new businesses in the future. Not all will work out. But this company began outside the oil and gas business. It had to go into a new business to get into the oil and gas business. As technology advances, there will likely be business opportunities that open up. As to what this company will look like in 100 years, it is up to speculation. However, the success in getting there is the key. So far, this company has been successful. The risk is that it may not be successful in the future in adjusting to whatever the future holds.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CoreWeave Vs. Nebius: Why One AI Cloud Stock Is Enough
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

Among neoclouds, I've been covering only Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ:NBIS) so far and didn't pay attention to CoreWeave, Inc. (NASDAQ:CRWV), which is a much bigger peer, and it's a pure-play U.S. neocloud that competes for basically the same customers and is exposed to the same cloud computing market (which is quite big, valued at ~$306.46 billion, and it's going to grow at a CAGR of over 20% for the next few years).

Anyway, from my perspective, there's no need to allocate capital to both of these to diversify, as it's going to eat away at the potential alpha. While I agree that CoreWeave is indeed a real titan of scale, a closer look at its financials and accompanying risks tells me that growth investors may find a more attractive and sustainable risk-to-reward growth opportunity in Nebius. So, although I have a Hold rating on both, I favor NBIS over CRWV because the former has a more diversified path to capturing the upside of the AI cloud market with a cleaner balance sheet and geo-outreach.

Why Do I Think So?

CoreWeave reported for its fiscal Q2 2025 in mid-August, and while it beat the top-line consensus by over 12% by delivering a 207% YoY expansion to ~$1.08 billion against the backdrop of $30.1 billion in committed contracts in its backlog, Wall Street analysts initially underestimated the firm's potential to shorten the "unprofitability gap." With a Q2 net loss of ~$290.5 million, mainly driven by ~$2.9 billion in CapEx and a quarterly interest expense of about $267 million, CRWV missed on the bottom line by over a third (~31.81%, according to Seeking Alpha). The market logically started to re-assess the high bar it had set on CRWV before the earnings print, and the stock dropped by over 33% since the Q2 publication:

There's no question about the underlying demand - as Goldman Sachs analysts put it in their post-earnings note to their clients (proprietary source, August 2025), CRWV's Q2 served as "further validation" that demand for the firm's infrastructure is actually quite robust. But again, the bar was initially set extremely high, so even that $30 billion plus in committed contracts wasn't enough. And the mounting net loss only exacerbated the negative narrative, raising reasonable questions about CoreWeave's business model sustainability.

I enjoyed reading Livy Investment Research's article on CRWV while preparing to write my own piece, and I highly recommend you go read it if you haven't already. In their research article, they state 4 overlooked risks, and I find all four to be quite reasonable.

One of the risks is CRWV's extreme customer concentration, with Microsoft (MSFT) alone accounting for ~62% of revenue as of FY2024, and CRWV's top two customers are at ~77%. So, I'm not suggesting that MSFT, OpenAI, and others will simply leave CoreWeave, but with a concentration like this, the company naturally loses its pricing power, especially given the future data center capacity expansion that may lead to underutilization (hence, lower rates per usage and lower revenues, all else being equal).

Another risk is what logically flows from the above one - CRWV's heavy debt load. For 1H FY2025 alone, CRWV's non-current debt on the balance sheet increased by ~$2.6 billion or ~52.2%, while its cash balance (including the current restricted cash) went up by only $314 million or 22.5% for the same period. If I divide select liability metrics by the $2.658 billion of their equity capital, I'll get way over 6x in the D/E ratio, and it's an alarming sign to me, given that CRWV keeps its massive CapEx program in place. If I divide the total liabilities by shareholders' equity, following the classic formula, I get 8.43x in debt-to-equity.

They basically purchase infrastructure assets against a signed customer contract, but the problem that Livy highlighted in their article is that those contracts may not be tied to specific generations of GPUs. So, in other words, the GPUs CoreWeave is investing billions in to date-risk rapid obsolescence and underutilization in a few years, and OpenAI can simply switch to fresher assets once the current contract ends, and this action would leave CRWV with tons of unneeded and outdated assets and massive debts.

I'm not saying that Nebius is immune to the above risk, but at least for now, it has virtually no debt, and its business model is much better diversified across non-infrastructure tech niches and geographies. As analyst Nate Endicott calculated in his X post a few days ago, both companies generate roughly $9-11 million in annualized revenue per active megawatt, which means that NBIS is monetizing its assets with comparable efficiency, having a better balance sheet, and riding diverse TAMs at the same time. And they target well beyond a handful of AI labs and hyperscalers with their ecosystem, so the entire ecosystem they run becomes effectively much more efficient. That's what I think the justification of Nebius' relative outperformance over the past few weeks, as CRWV has been losing its post-IPO momentum:

Another thing that I don't understand in CRWV's case is their scaling plan. On paper, it sounds good overall, but as we found out, the financing method they chose led to a more shaky risk profile, and the firm didn't bother to try to break even. Even NBIS, which is only getting started, is planning to secure over 1 GW of power by the end of 2026 and a $2 billion CapEx plan for 2025, and all that amid an EBITDA breakeven. When it comes to CRWV, it looks like they're now trying to turn a profit, but the interest expense eats away all their efforts, and so the breakeven path becomes much more complicated for them.

Now, let's turn to the valuation picture, as it seems to be misleading some investors when they decide between NBIS and CRWV.

It's clear that NBIS is much more expensive in terms of conventional valuation multiples, such as FWD price-to-sales (they exceed CRWV's multiple by a factor of ~2.8):

But again, CoreWeave's breakeven plan seems to be slowly shifting: Although they should be greatly EBITDA positive and they hold a FWD EV/EBITDA of ~20x, they have no FWD P/E GAAP, based on Seeking Alpha. At the same time, NBIS holds a forward GAAP P/E ratio of 73.46x right now, and while it seems to be high, the growth pace they've got is what can justify a very high valuation multiple.

In 2 years from now, the current consensus sees CRWV's P/E at 44.7x, while Nebius' P/E for FY2027 can't be properly calculated because of the existing lack of consensus estimates beyond 2026. But from my understanding, the miss we saw in CRWV's Q2 net loss per share suggests that the current consensus regarding its FY2027 EPS is actually quite unsustainable. At the same time, once Nebius breaks even on EBITDA on a full-year basis, its zero-debt status should enable it to gain great operating leverage and show a real profit much sooner than even bulls can think of. So, for me, the valuation comparison is actually also pointing to Nebius' superiority (although I still think the stock is overvalued - take a look at my previous article on the topic).

The Bottom Line

It might seem that my article turned out to be one-directional, meaning that in all points touched, I was preferring NBIS over CRWV. But I should say that I wish that NBIS had CoreWeave's scale right now (not in 2-3 years), and I like the addressable market specialization CRWV holds right now (I'm talking about their U.S. presence primarily and the relationships they've built with large hyperscalers). Anyway, on a net-net basis, Nebius is simply more sustainable in the longer term, and that's why its FWD valuation premium doesn't evaporate the way CRWV's valuation multiples do.

For those who already own Nebius, adding CoreWeave isn't really a diversification play. In my view, it's just an overlap in basically the same end market (probably with a better U.S. positioning, but still), but CRWV has a riskier profile and naturally worse risk/reward for growth investors.

I'm happy to keep holding NBIS, and while I did trim a lot because I was overexposed to it, it's great to see that the stock is holding up well against the tech space in general and against its direct peers such as CRWV in particular. The most likely outcome for the next few years is that this outperformance continues. But for now, I'm waiting for a pullback in NBIS to add more to it, leaving my Hold in place. I decided to rate CRWV as Hold as well this time.

Please share your thoughts in the comments section below (probably you have a different view on this CRWV/NBIS comparison).

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Palantir: Never Doubt Leadership Like This (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since my last Palantir (PLTR) analysis, the stock has gained 11.5% in price. In my last analysis, I sold my position. In this piece, I've bought it back, at double the weight as before. Sometimes it takes time to know what's right for you.

First things first. We should tackle the elephant in the room. Almost every analyst is talking about Palantir's valuation (they have been for three years). "It's too high," "hold." However, any student of this game that knows how to win in real life understands that a stock price tomorrow doesn't care about the market's feelings today. Let me say that a bit clearer: what the market thinks is correct today, is not what is going to happen a year from now, or a decade from now. More simply: people (even professionals) are often very wrong.

So what is it about Palantir that makes it stand out and allows it to defy the natural order of things? Why can this company trade at a forward P/E ratio of nearly 250 and still go up, while other companies wouldn't last months or even days at that valuation? The answer is in the leadership.

Visionary Leadership Begets Durable Ultra-Premium Valuations

There are two pivotal figures that stand out in Palantir's leadership that define the current return longevity. There are clearly others, I have heard some speak at length, and the whole team ought to be given credit. But first of all, let's recognize the immeasurable influence of Peter Thiel—quite possibly one of the greatest technology investors of all time. As one of the founders, Thiel is also Chairman of the Board, a role he has had since co-founding the company in 2003. Admittedly, Thiel isn't in Palantir's offices making product decisions, but he has the ear of Alex Karp, the company's famous CEO. He is the second pivotal figure I allude to, and we'll get to him in just a moment. Thiel's network in U.S. politics and defense circles is second to none in Silicon Valley—to be clear, Thiel has always framed Palantir as a company defending Western civilization against external threats, and Palantir has developed a de facto reputation for aligning with U.S. and allied geopolitical interests. This doesn't just help the company's government-related revenue growth, it also improves commercial strategy given that Fortune 500 CEOs benefit from lower-risk adoption from a compliance and political standpoint. I believe Thiel is a visionary, and he is guiding Palantir with a second-to-none vision in its field. The fundamentals match this vision (a 30–40% long-term forward EPS and revenue CAGR on consensus is elite by almost all standards).

Karp has also operationally and managerially embodied the founder-led contrarianism that the company was established on—not doing what is popular, but doing what is right (and what works). Field-deployed engineering (also known by the company by the role of Forward Deployed Engineer), whereby Palantir deploys its staff onto customer sites, also proves the hands-on attitude that separates Palantir from your typical technology development and consultancy company. In this way, Palantir acts more like a military intelligence division, and the market pays not only for the results of this lean, agile operating model, but also for the narrative of its direction under Karp operationally and Thiel meta-operationally. Remove or weaken either narrator, and the current valuation multiples would not realistically be sustained until the processes prove they can carry the story (that would mean much slower and less visionary stock price growth, not to mention lower company morale—so the flywheel between strong leadership and operational excellence is indispensable).

Palantir's Valuation Is Fundamentally Sustainable

When valuing Palantir, I've argued for a long time that it's truly useless to attempt peer analysis and intrinsic value analysis, as you will be missing the sustainable sentiment-driven returns that we have witnessed over the past few years and will likely continue to witness for the foreseeable future. Instead, buying and holding Palantir at a reasonable sentiment-derived valuation based on near-term averages is enough of a gauge to secure alpha. With a company as strong as this with recurring 30–40% top and bottom line annual growth rates on consensus moving forward, you don't need to overthink it. This is a world-class company that is set for enduring alpha as fundamentals continue to compound aggressively upwards, and the high valuation multiples will simply drag on total annual returns rather than cause severe contraction (the business model is too resilient for a major crisis to cause indefinite downside, though there is always the potential for the improbable to become manifest).

As you can see from the above chart, PLTR is trading just along the 50-day moving average with an RSI of about 50. This indicates that the stock is essentially fairly valued right now from a sentiment perspective. Given this fair valuation and the durability of ~30% fundamental growth in the medium to long term (5–10 years), we can rationally assume a 30% CAGR for this stock over the long term. This leans slightly more bullish than my prior research, and there is the risk of valuation multiple compression if growth even slightly moderates, but the low end of the rational spectrum of return potential is about a 20% long-term price CAGR, with 30% the base case and 40% the bull. That's an incredibly rich return horizon, and it makes me wonder, why not own PLTR?

Three crucial elements validate the fundamental return horizon:

  1. Firstly, Palantir has Net Dollar Retention of ~128%, meaning existing customers expand ~28% annually. New logos only need to add 2–12% to hit 30–40%.
  2. Secondly, Palantir's AI Bootcamps are compressing adoption to five days, pulling total contract value, billings, and revenue in faster.
  3. Thirdly, U.S. commercial revenue grew +93% year-over-year in Q2 2025, which signals a remarkable transfer of government credibility into enterprise adoption. Sustained U.S. commercial outperformance validates scalability.

Risks To Thesis

There are many micro risks that attentive analysts can uncover, but I'm a believer in paying attention to detail but not losing direction because of imperfections. Palantir's macro direction, and the positioning of its stock, is certainly elite.

The stock trades at ~90x forward sales with a ~250x forward P/E. That valuation assumes flawless execution and means that any slip like growth slowdown, contract conversion lag, a hiring bottleneck, or hyperscaler encroachment, could very easily lead to multiple compression, substantially slowing the stock price CAGR (in a tail-risk worst-case scenario, to below 20% annually).

This is a high-risk investment to some because the company's valuation is essentially trading at the moment on a one-strike policy (in theory). That means a small execution miss could lead to a large repricing. In reality, those who fear such an event miss the magnitude of Palantir's lead, network moat, and structural development trajectory, let alone its leadership (the defining factor of this thesis).

Additionally, the Army Enterprise Agreement of "up to $10 billion over 10 years" is a maximum cap, not committed spending. Realized revenue only comes from funded task orders, but the AI adoption trajectory is so strong that I find it unlikely that these caps won't be substantially exhausted. However, historical precedent shows that big government contracts like these for other companies are not intended to reach the full amount. Palantir itself admits in its filings that the big contract values it reports assume every option gets used, which rarely happens. The equity is trading like ceilings are backlogged, but the nuance here suggests there's a value premium more tied to narrative right now than structurally defined fundamental growth. I'm not saying that growth won't materialize; I think it will, but you still have to realize you're still paying heavily for long-term narrative at this stage and at this valuation. Personally, I don't have a problem with that; bring it on.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

Palantir stock is high-risk and high-reward, but if you're seeking world-class returns, that's what you want. At least, in the case of Palantir, the perception is that the stock is high-risk, but in reality, because of the quality of leadership and the secular AI growth trajectory, we're really looking at a moderate-risk investment with probable high alpha. Often you can look at management as one of the first proxies for long-term company success, and in this instance, the flags are all green. Look past the valuation; Palantir is worth long-term holding, and I see that now.

data.title: Celsius Holdings: PepsiCo Partnership And Major Acquisitions Fuel Good Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Celsius Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CELH) shares surged after the announcement of strengthening the partnership with PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP). There are three major news items: first, Alani Nu joins the PepsiCo distribution system. Secondly, PepsiCo has increased its ownership in Celsius to 11%, and thirdly, it has acquired the Rockstar Energy brand in the U.S. and Canada. Celsius is a Buy for me, and I still see an upside of 20%.

Rise, fall, and rise again

Celsius Holdings has already seen a steep rise since its low of $22 in February this year. However, when I bought in the low $30 range, the stock had declined by almost 70% from its all-time high. Celsius shares were overhyped and overvalued. If stocks are priced to perfection, bad news can cause a bloodbath. And that is exactly what happened to Celsius when PepsiCo adjusted its inventory after buying too much. My rationale was simple: the fear among investors that PepsiCo might end the partnership was a very unrealistic scenario, because energy is a growing category, and it wouldn't make sense for PepsiCo to reduce its share. Inventory adjustments must end eventually; therefore, revenue growth will pick up again.

The narrative has changed, and Celsius is now stronger than before. The acquisition of Alani Nu, a female-focused energy drink, for $1.8 billion was a big surprise to me. I saw Alani as the biggest competitor for Celsius, and with the acquisition, the biggest risk turned into a major catalyst. Alani Nu could benefit from Celsius's scale, and together both brands now hold a market share of 18%. Accounting for more than 50% of total energy category growth, Celsius offers the fastest-growing portfolio in the category.

But let's talk about the most recent news.

Alani Nu joins the distribution network of PepsiCo

In 2022, Celsius and PepsiCo announced a long-term distribution deal. Revenues doubled in each of the following two years, rising from $314 million to $1.3 billion. The partnership expanded Celsius's availability and provided access to new channels such as food service. To be a successful beverage brand, distribution is key. For example, Monster Beverage Corporation (MNST) has The Coca-Cola Company (KO) as a partner and, with its help, has grown into the second-largest energy drink company worldwide.

It also makes perfect sense to expand distribution for Alani Nu. According to the most recent presentation, Celsius Holdings expects to see an acceleration in distribution reach in already existing channels such as mass market and grocery, as well as other strategic channels like food service. Currently, Alani Nu has a distribution reach of 87% in grocery + convenience and only 69% in convenience stores. Compared to Celsius's 98%, there is still upside for Alani Nu. I expect Alani Nu to reach a similar distribution level within the next two years. This creates more points of access for customers and could drive sales. I don't expect Alani Nu's sales to jump another 100% like Celsius's, since an 87% distribution reach is already high. In 2024, Alani Nu's net sales were $605 million, growing at a CAGR of 49% between 2022 and 2024. I think that expanded distribution will help Alani Nu maintain these massive growth rates for some time before they slow down. It is also possible that growth accelerates, but as mentioned, I consider that unlikely.

Celsius becomes PepsiCo's strategic energy drink captain

PepsiCo increased its ownership in Celsius to 11% after investing an additional $585 million in newly issued 5% convertible preferred stock. Moreover, both beverage companies aim to strengthen their strategic alignment. That's good to see, especially after last year's investor concerns that PepsiCo might take the partnership less seriously or even end it.

PepsiCo will be responsible for distribution, while Celsius Holdings has commercial control over the energy portfolio strategy and execution. Before this agreement, PepsiCo and Celsius worked together less efficiently.

Following today's agreement, both companies will unify their strategic direction over portfolio retail planograms, SKU prioritization, and promotional execution. This means that both companies jointly will control how products appear on shelves, stock-outs will be reduced because of better communication, and campaigns won't be fragmented across the brands in the portfolio anymore. This allows retailers to run targeted activations, e.g., fitness-focused with Celsius and mainstream with Rockstar, without an overlap.

I believe that PepsiCo is outsourcing its energy strategy to Celsius. It's a good sign that PepsiCo has such strong trust in the execution capabilities of Celsius's management. The narrative from a couple of months ago has changed, and the strategic partnership between the companies is now stronger than ever before.

Additionally, I expect to see some margin improvements. PepsiCo's support in buying out existing distributors of Alani Nu and streamlining production for both brands will improve margins. Moreover, the inventory misalignment that occurred between both companies months ago is now less likely to happen because of better collaboration. I also believe we will see further efficiency gains, as promotions will be better timed across the energy portfolio. In general, it's beneficial for a beverage company to reach a bigger scale, as it helps in negotiations with retailers and drives costs down.

Celsius Holdings has evolved from a single-brand company to a portfolio of energy drink brands supported by a strong partnership.

Celsius acquires Rockstar Energy

The most surprising part of the announcement was the acquisition of Rockstar Energy in the U.S. and Canada. The holding now includes three brands: Celsius is the zero-sugar fitness lifestyle functional energy drink. Alani Nu is a female-focused energy drink. Rockstar, however, is a classic energy drink, especially popular with gamers. The total market share of these three brands combined is 20%, which makes Celsius Holdings the third-largest player within the energy category.

I am still trying to understand the strategic rationale behind this move. Rockstar Energy was acquired in 2020 by PepsiCo for $3.85 billion. The Rockstar brand suffered for years. The rise of Monster Beverage, which became a serious competitor, as well as the shift towards healthier options impacting traditional energy drink sales led by Celsius, put pressure on Rockstar, causing it to lose market share and popularity.

I believe that the primary reason for the acquisition is that PepsiCo wants to outsource its energy portfolio to Celsius Holdings. The acquisition price was $585 million, which corresponds exactly to the $585 million in new preferred convertible shares that PepsiCo received. It's less of an acquisition and more of a trade of cards.

Another reason could be that Celsius Holdings wants to revitalize the Rockstar brand. Celsius has achieved its position in the energy space by advertising its products in an unconventional and modern way. Marketing is influencer-led and community-driven. Gen Z creators like Jake Paul represent the brand, and Celsius has several million followers on Instagram and TikTok.

Celsius wants to revitalize the Rockstar brand with its marketing expertise, its strong sales infrastructure, and cross-brand collaboration, according to management.

We will see if the acquisition turns out well. Now, I'm more neutral. I don't think Celsius has overpaid. Celsius has paid less than the retail sales of Rockstar, which were $643 million, and just 2.3 times actual sales. Compared to the nearly $4 billion PepsiCo has paid for Rockstar, the deal was much less expensive. I also expect Celsius to have a positive impact on Rockstar. However, because we have no information about margins, cash flows, and a more detailed outlay of the strategy, it is too early to say if this acquisition is a big win for the platform. One positive thing for sure is that there is minimal consumer overlap. Moreover, PepsiCo and Celsius are now more aligned after this deal, which could have a net positive effect.

Valuation

It is better to use the P/Sales ratio than the P/E for Celsius. The company has now acquired two other brands and is scaling the whole platform. If we take the earnings per share, the forward P/E of 60 is very expensive. But I think that this number is not accurate enough, because margins will expand for Alani Nu and perhaps also Rockstar over time. The bigger scale and synergies will impact the acquired brands positively. For example, the gross profit of Alani Nu is 42% compared to the 50% of Celsius in 2024, and the income from operations is 11% of revenue compared to 20% in 2023. I use the 2023 figures, as the last year was impacted by the one-time inventory misalignment between Celsius and PepsiCo. It will make more sense to value the business with the P/E ratio when it is more mature and the other two brands are fully integrated.

Monster Beverage is the only other listed energy drink company, as Red Bull is privately held. Monster Beverage's revenue in 2024 was around $7.5 billion, and the forward price-to-sales ratio is 7.58. Investors are willing to pay more than 7.5 times the revenue for the business.

I also believe that the sector median of 1.18 is misleading. Celsius Holdings is a business in the consumer staples sector. However, the energy drink category is outpacing other categories in the sector. For example, the soft drink category is projected to grow at a CAGR of 5% between 2025 and 2030. The energy drink category is projected to grow at a CAGR of 8% over the same period. Moreover, Celsius is outperforming the sector median in various metrics. For instance, Celsius has a revenue growth of 11.85% compared to the sector median of 2.09% and a gross margin of 50.53% compared to the sector median of 35.52%. I have the data from Seeking Alpha directly.

Analysts expect the revenue of Celsius Holdings to reach $2.36 billion this year. If we apply an 8 times P/Sales ratio, we get a market cap of $19 billion. That's an upside of over 20% and a price target of $75. I know that the current price is not cheap anymore. However, I think that Celsius deserves a premium compared to Monster Beverage, as Celsius Holdings now contributes to more than 50% of category growth. The forward price/sales ratio is only 6.6 compared to the 7.58 of Monster. Moreover, in 2021, the price-to-sales ratio even exceeded 20 times, and the forward multiple of under 7 is way below that. We can also add the Rockstar revenue of $250 million to the $2.36 billion. A multiple of 8 results in a market cap of over $20.18 billion, an upside of more than 30%.

Risks

First, execution risk. Celsius has acquired Alani Nu for $1.8 billion and Rockstar for $585 million this year. It appears that the market currently prices in flawless execution, and difficulties in integration or weakening demand for both acquired brands could change the narrative, causing the stock to go south.

Furthermore, the stock is up over 150% year-to-date. Although I believe that Celsius still has some upside potential, there is also room for a bigger correction, as some investors could realize their gains. Moreover, there is a gap in the chart, which would mean a potential downside to $42.5 if the gap gets closed.

Moreover, the Rockstar acquisition could have a negative impact on profitability. The ratio of retail sales to actual sales of Rockstar is around 39%, compared to 50% of the Celsius platform. This indicates that Rockstar is a lower-margin brand than the other two. However, I believe that Celsius could stabilize the Rockstar brand and improve the brand's margin.

Summary

Celsius is not on sale anymore, as it was several months ago. Nevertheless, I think Celsius still has upside potential of 20%, excluding the impact of Rockstar Energy. Competitor Monster Beverage grows at a much slower rate but is valued similarly to Celsius, which is leading category growth with its platform. The company now combines three energy drink brands and could benefit from scale, synergies, and a stronger relationship and better strategic alignment with PepsiCo.

data.title: ARGT: Still Strong Fundamentals, But Political And FX Volatility Change The Call (Downgrade)
data.description:
In my previous June article ("ARGT ETF Advances In Line With Argentine Macroeconomic Stabilization") I argued that the ETF Global X MSCI Argentina (NYSEARCA:ARGT) was one of the best ways to participate in the new Argentinian cycle, with solid foundations: fiscal surplus, rapid disinflation, and firms in full transformation.

Three months later, I see that those fundamentals are holding, the companies continue to perform well, but the short-term dynamics have become more complex.

The intervention of the Treasury in the FX market, the disarming of the LeFi, the volatility of interest rates, and the increasing political noise of the last few weeks lead me to soften the vision and move the position from Buy to Hold.

From FX Calm To Treasury Intervention

For much of 2024 and early 2025, the government’s strongest signal was not to intervene directly in the foreign exchange market. The stability of the financial dollar within the AR$ 1000-1400 band was a pillar of credibility.

However, in recent days the Treasury began to sell currency to contain the pressure on the peso. Reuters described it as:

Argentina’s Treasury intervened in FX markets to stabilize the peso, marking a shift from the government’s hands-off approach.

I think this decision may be seen as a short-term pragmatic gesture, but it raises doubts about the soundness of an anchor FX without discretionary intervention.

Disarming LeFi And The New Rate Volatility

The Central Bank (BCRA) decided to discontinue the Financing Letters (LeFi), a sterilization instrument that functioned as remunerated liabilities, and transfer those positions into Treasury bonds.

The Buenos Aires Herald clearly detailed it:

From July 10, Fiscal Liquidity Letters (LEFIs) will no longer be issued... to be replaced with Treasury short-term bills (LECAPs)

The stated objective was to reduce the quasi-fiscal deficit and clean up the BCRA’s balance sheet, but the immediate consequence was a tightening of liquidity. Banks reacted by raising deposit rates, which today are between 50% and 58%.

Bloomberg summed it up as:

a necessary but destabilizing move in the transition from central bank to treasury sterilization.

For investors, this means a more volatile market in pesos and therefore greater sensitivity of financial assets to each news item.

Policy Predicts The Markets

Politics never ceases to be news in Argentina. Congress passed a provisional law contrary to the Executive’s position, with negative effects on the fiscal surplus, and blocked parts of the reform agenda.

This was compounded by potential acts of corruption, which increased institutional uncertainty. The result was a jump in country risk, again in the area of 800-830 bps.

The Buenos Aires Times summarized it as follows:

Political turbulence and a new pension bill increased uncertainty, lifting country risk and hurting local markets.

In an ETF like ARGT, highly concentrated on a few companies, such shocks translate directly into sharp price movements.

IMF: support on more lenient terms

The International Monetary Fund (IMF) approved the first revision of the program, disbursed $2B, and, most importantly, relaxed reserve accumulation targets and postponed the next evaluation until after the elections.

The IMF Staff Report put it in these terms:

"The IMF adjusted reserve targets and delayed the next review until after the elections, underscoring execution risks.

This provides oxygen in the short term, but also reflects that conditions are more fragile than expected and that the continuity of the programme will depend on the political outcome.

Disinflation Resists, But Does Not Dispel All Doubts

Monthly inflation, which had been 25% monthly in December 2023, fell to levels of 1.5-2% in May and June.

Without a doubt, I observe that the trend is clear: the Argentine economy is in a process of disinflation, product of fiscal adjustment and monetary contraction.

However, the latest data show us some pressure on prices due to tariff correction and FX. I do not see it as a trend break, but as a reminder that the path to low inflation is not linear.

Activity, meanwhile, showed a rebound in the second quarter of 2025, although there remain doubts about its sustainability in a scenario of higher rates and political volatility.

ARGT: Large Vehicle, But More Dependent On Holders

ARGT remains the most efficient way to access Argentine equity from abroad. Its portfolio includes weight names such as YPF, Vista (VIST), Pampa Energía (PAM), Galicia (GGAL), BBVA Argentina (BBAR) and MercadoLibre (MELI), all with solid results in 2025.

But around 70-75% of its positions are concentrated in just 10 companies, which makes it very sensitive to the economic situation.

Today it is trading between $77 and $82, in the middle range of its annual trajectory ($51-$96), a level where news flow weighs more than corporate fundamentals.

Conclusion: From Buy To Hold

I have no doubt that the long-term fundamentals of ARGT remain attractive: a country looking to get its books in order, with companies improving balance sheets and increasing dividends.

But in the short term, FX intervention, interest rate volatility after LeFi disarming, and political noise broaden the range of possible scenarios. I think ARGT remains a valuable instrument, but prudence today suggests holding positions rather than increasing them.

My change of stance to Hold reflects just that: conviction in the long-term potential, but caution until legislative elections clear the picture.

data.title: U.S. Jobs Data Key To Greenback's Near-Term Fate
data.description:
Overview

The US dollar (DXY) is firm against the G10 currencies today but is mostly trading inside yesterday's ranges. After yesterday's disappointing JOLTS report, attention turns to the ADP estimate today, ahead of tomorrow's BLS report. The lack of follow-through selling after yesterday's losses may be encouraging some short-term momentum traders to move to the sidelines. The greenback is also mostly higher against emerging market currencies, though a few Asia-Pacific currencies are bucking the move, including a small gain for the Chinese yuan.

The pullback in yields seen yesterday is continuing today. Steady demand at Japan's 30-year bond auction helped JGBs rally. European benchmark yields are off 3-5 bp with spreads over Germany narrowing slightly, including France. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) has slipped below 4.20%. Equities, following the gains in the S&P and Nasdaq yesterday, are mostly higher. The notable exceptions are China and Hong Kong, following reports that suggested Beijing officials are considering measures to cool the recent rally. Europe's STOXX 600 (STOXX) is building on yesterday's 0.65% recovery after Tuesday's 1.5% slide. US index futures are trading with a firmer bias as well. After surging to a record $3578.5 yesterday, profit-taking sent gold down a little through $3512 today before buyers re-emerged. October WTI is extending its pullback after briefly trading above $66 on Tuesday. It fell to almost $63.70 yesterday and nearly $63.00 today.

USD: The disappointing July JOLTS report pushed what was an offered Dollar Index lower. It had peaked earlier slightly above Tuesday's high, slightly shy of 98.65, and returned to almost 98.00. It is consolidating today in a narrow range mostly between 98.10 and 98.35. Still, when everything is said and done, with one brief exception of Monday's low, the Dollar Index remains within the range set on August 22, when Fed chief Powell spoke at Jackson Hole (~97.55-98.85). There is a slew of US economic reports today. Given that the deterioration of the labor market is behind the elevated chances of a Fed rate cut later this month, the ADP private sector job estimate may be the most important. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey stands at 68k, down from 104k in July. Given the US tariff offensive, the July trade balance will also draw attention. The advanced goods deficit widened by 22% to $103.6 bln. Imports jumped 7.1% and exports slipped by 0.1%. The overall trade deficit was $582.7 bln in H1 '25, up from $421.2 bln in H1 '24, though distorted by front-running the tariffs. The ISM services report is more important than the final services and composite PMI. The ISM services were slightly above 50 in July, while the services PMI was at 55.7 (before slipping to 55.4 in August, according to the preliminary estimate). Lastly, the accelerated confirmation hearings of Miran to the Federal Reserve will be held today before the Senate Banking Committee. Despite his earlier work, which includes sketching out how the executive could control the central bank, Miran's prepared remarks pledge to defend the independence of the Federal Reserve. What could go wrong?

Euro: The euro held above $1.1600 yesterday and recovered to poke a little above $1.1680 in North America. With the exception of last Wednesday's low (~$1.1575), the euro has also been confined to the range set on August 22 (~$1.1585-1.1745). It is in about a third of a cent range below $1.1670 so far today. The broad sideways movement has wreaked havoc with the daily momentum indicators. We note that the US two-year premium over Germany is a new low for the year, slightly below 165 bp. Last year's low was around 135 bp. It was above 200 bp as recently as late July. The eurozone reported a 0.5% decline in July retail sales, which was a bit larger than the 0.3% drag expected by the median forecast in Bloomberg's survey. Yet, it should not be surprising. Germany (-1.5%), France (-0.6%), and Spain (-0.4%) had already reported their figures. Italy reports its figures tomorrow.

CNY: The dollar held below CNH7.15 on Tuesday and Wednesday. It traded inside Tuesday's range yesterday in a consolidative session and is trading within yesterday's range today. If the PBOC signaled through the setting of the dollar's reference rate that it did not want the yuan to rise much more, then it may be challenged if the greenback continues to weaken. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate higher for the third consecutive session yesterday, matching the longest run in five months. Given the dollar's heavier tone, especially in North America yesterday, the PBOC had little choice but to lower the dollar's reference rate today (CNY7.1052 vs. CNY7.1108 yesterday). Two developments are noteworthy today. First, while the Federal Reserve protects it independence, China's Vice Finance Minister and the Deputy Governor of the PBOC met today and committed to closer cooperation to support the economy. Many suspect that loose monetary policy can help support more public spending to strengthen growth. Second, Chinese financial regulators are considering measures to temper the stock market rally, which could include the removal of some short selling restrictions. The mere fact that it is being discussed weighed on Chinese equities today, sending the CSI 300 down 2.1%, the biggest loss in five months.

JPY: The weak JOLTS report pushed US rates lower and dragged the dollar off its highs against the yen. The 30-year Treasury yield got as close to 5.0% without trading there, before falling back below 4.90% to approach last week's settlement slightly below 4.88%. The 10-year Treasury yield dropped ten basis points to fray the 4.20% yield, the lower end of what has been the range since early May. The dollar has reached nearly JPY149.15 in late Asia/early Europe hours but never traded above JPY148.80 in North America, before falling below JPY148. It made a marginal new low today near JPY147.80 but recovered to about JPY148.40 in early European turnover. Japan reports July labor cash earnings tomorrow. They are hovering around 3% year-over-year. Yet, when adjusted for inflation, they have mostly been falling, with a few exceptions, like the end and middle of last year. Yet, consumer spending has been positive though weak, averaging a little more than 0.6% over the past three quarters at an annualized rate through Q2 '25. July household spending will be reported at the same time. It is expected to have accelerated to 2.3% year-over-year from 1.3% in June. If so, it would be the third consecutive advance, the longest in three years.

GBP: Neither vulnerability of the Deputy Prime Minister Rayner over unpaid taxes on a property purchase, the announcement of what seems to be an unusual late Autumn Budget Statement (November 26), nor the looming fiscal challenge was sufficient to hold sterling back yesterday. Indeed, sterling rivalled the Australian dollar for the top performance yesterday among the G10 currencies. Follow-through selling from Tuesday initially took it to a new low since August 6 slightly below $1.3335. From there it recovered to almost $1.3460 new session highs, which held today. Initial resistance is seen in the $1.3470-85 area. The $1.3400-20 area offers support. The market did not respond much to the UK's new car registrations (a proxy for auto sales). They were off 2% after falling 5% year-over-year in July. The August construction PMI ticked up to 45.3 (from 44.3) but has not been above the 50 boom/bust level this year.

CAD: In the better-offered greenback, the Canadian dollar underperformed. It was the weakest of the G10 currencies, losing a miniscule 0.1%. Still, the greenback barely traded below Tuesday's settlement yesterday, while it held below Tuesday's high (~CAD1.3815) in lackluster activity. The US dollar is a little firmer today and threatens to extend its advancing streak for the fourth consecutive session. It is at the week's high in Europe and is probing the lower band of resistance seen in the CAD1.3825-50 area. Given the importance of the trade drag on Q2 GDP, today's July merchandise trade figures will be closely watched. Canada recorded a C$19 bln goods deficit in Q2, swelling from a little less than C$400 mln deficit in Q1. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for a C$5.3 bln shortfall in July. In July 2024, the deficit was a little more than C$320 mln.

AUD: The Australian dollar traded well below $0.6500 on Tuesday but held above it yesterday and rallied to almost $0.6555. Around half of the recovery occurred in the European morning, and the other half when North Americans entered the fray. It is trading with a heavier bias today but still inside yesterday's range, which was inside Tuesday's range (~$0.6485-0.6560). Australia reported its July trade balance earlier today. It defied expectations of narrowing, and instead, swelled to A$7.3 bln after surging to almost A$5.4 bln in June (from A$1.6 bln in May). Exports rose by 5.1% on the month, while imports fell 1.3%. Today's July figures showed household spending increased 0.5% in July, for a 5.1% year-over-year pace, the strongest in almost two years. Although Australian interest rates eased today, central bank governor Bullock noted after the stronger GDP figures earlier this week that the data was stronger than officials expected, and noted that if it continues, "there may not be many interest rate declines yet to come."

MXN: Despite the greenback's struggle yesterday, the peso was unable to capitalize. The US dollar traded well within Tuesday's session in mostly featureless activity. Yesterday's low was recorded in response to the JOLTS report, a little below MXN18.66. The greenback's recovery ran out of steam in front of MXN18.73 but rose to almost MXN18.7650 today. Tuesday's range, roughly MXN18.6225-18.8635, still dominates. Mexico reports June fixed investment and private consumption, July leading economic indicators, and August auto sales. While they may be useful data points for economists, they probably do not capture the imagination or attention of market participants. US jobs data and the broad direction of the dollar are more salient drivers of the exchange rate.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Asset Entities: 'Yes' Vote For The Win - Strong Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The next few weeks are critical for Asset Entities (ASST) stock. The SEC, back in August, declared the upcoming merger with Strive allowed. Now, everything depends on a special shareholder meeting that will take place on September 9th to decide if the merger goes through or not. Here’s the catch: about 40% of the vote has already been secured in favor of the merger via the company board. With a majority needed to rule, they require just an additional 11% from shareholders for the merger to proceed. If Asset Entities were to merge with Strive, shares would continue trading under the same ticker and should undergo a significant revaluation upwards. Hence, I'm maintaining my strong buy rating on the stock as I see odds lined up in favor of this going through.

In my previous coverage of Asset Entities, I initiated with a strong buy rating, and while we saw the stock go through large upward swings, I will admit I published my buy call a little too early. The stock is up 68% on the one-month chart but is down around 6% since my strong buy. With the merger likely to go through, I feel the market can now properly value this company as a Bitcoin treasury moving forward, and I see the stock price reacting positively. The good news is that the company still holds over $750 million via the previous PIPE investment, ready to open up a position in Bitcoin at the right time. Other companies, such as Sharplink Gaming (SBET), have gone through similar transition periods, which saw their stock trading at much higher premiums after the move to a crypto treasury. Asset Entities is still behind in terms of their timeline for the transition; they’re yet to own any crypto. However, after next week, we could see them take a very big step towards becoming a treasury.

Financials

For the most recent quarter, the company brought in just $173,000 in 2Q25, a figure that is up 86% year over year. For the first half of 2025, revenue was $344,000, up 58% from $218,000 in the same period last year. EPS was around a $0.17 loss, much improved from the $0.58 loss last year. If we look at these numbers alone, growth is improving, yes, but their revenue is still tiny compared to total costs. Operating expenses came in at $2.87 million vs. the $1.82 million spent in the same period last year. G&A expense was about $936,000, up 24% year over year, and management comp was $1.8 million, up 90% since 2Q24. Contract labour stayed steady at roughly $132,000. In terms of cash, the company still holds $2.52 million, with total assets at $3.26 million. The bottom line here is that while still tiny, Asset Entities is growing. We’re seeing a heavy spend on management comp and G&A continue, which is driving those losses, but equity raisings are plugging that gap; stock issuance contributed $3.1 million in 1H25 alone. They hold a very thin cash cushion for their kind of business at just 2.5 million. I don’t see this lasting long without new financing.

An Upcoming Merger

The most recent news out of Asset Entities is that the merger with Strive Enterprises was declared effective by the SEC on August 22nd of last month. The combined company will have a name change, now called Strive, Inc., but it will continue trading under the ticker ASST. Any Strive shareholders, including equity award holders, will own about 94.2% of the merged entity. It leaves Asset Entities holding about 5.8%. Matt Cole, the CEO of Strive, will become chairman of the combined company, with Arshia Sarkhani, the Asset Entities CEO, becoming CMO and board member.

Asset Entities also filed an S-4 registration statement with the SEC. It’s a registration statement that companies must file with the SEC when issuing new shares in connection with a merger or acquisition. The SEC declared this effective on the 22nd, which means regulators have finished their review, and Asset Entities are now legally allowed to move forward with soliciting shareholder votes. The company will be able to issue new shares if the merger is approved. Keep in mind that this does not mean the SEC endorses the deal; it simply means the disclosure is sufficient and investors have the full information needed to vote. That brings us to the upcoming shareholder vote next week on September 9th. Because the merger involves issuing new shares to Strive’s shareholders, Asset Entities’ shareholders must, of course, first approve. The company has set a record date of July 21st of this year, so only investors who owned shares as of that date can vote. The Asset Entities board has already lined up roughly 40% of the voting power in favour of issuing shares. Typically, the merger would require approval from a simple majority of 51%. That leaves them needing just an additional 11% to pass. If approved, the merger will close fully, leaving Asset Entities shareholders with just 5.8% of the combined company. Strive is, of course, much larger. The good news is that this is Asset Entities' window to become a public Bitcoin Treasury. They still hold about $750 million from the PIPE financing, and a Section 351 exchange is planned. Cole recently took to X to post about the upcoming vote, recommending that shareholders vote ‘yes.’

On the flip side, if the vote doesn't pass, the merger won’t close. It would leave Asset Entities back on its own again with its current small-scale business and a very limited cash reserve. Given that fragile balance sheet, I expect any failure to merge would put significant pressure on Asset Entities and the stock. I see shares moving negatively if the vote doesn’t pass. However, with 40% of the vote already locked in, management only needs a modest margin of additional support. I expect the merger to get approved, and we should see Asset Entities transition from a small marketing company with under $3 million in assets to a Bitcoin Treasury play with over $1 billion in crypto.

A Position In Bitcoin

If the merger were to go through, how well-positioned are Asset Entities to capitalize on a Bitcoin position? Well, the company plans a Section 351 Exchange, which would immediately transfer Bitcoin into the new entity and make it one of the top 100 corporate Bitcoin treasuries. At that point, we would see Bitcoin as the primary asset on the company's balance sheet next quarter, not cash flow from a business. Their main lever would, of course, be the value of Bitcoin itself. So profitability would have depended on Bitcoin going up. At that point, similar to other treasuries, the stock price will fluctuate based on the value of the crypto the treasury holds. We know they hold about $750 million for the PIPE, so they're ready to accumulate Bitcoin aggressively should the price be right. We may even see them issue debt or equity in the future, similar to what MicroStrategy (MSTR) has done in the past to buy more crypto. The CFO of Strive recently stated on X that the company is ready to start buying crypto as soon as the voting is passed.

So, at current Bitcoin prices of $110,000, the company could assume a total of 13,636 Bitcoin, given it has $750 million from the PIPE and an additional $750 million from warrant exercises. And with a current market cap of just $102 million, we would see the company’s value multiply sharply. I modeled the company's valuation against future Bitcoin price movements to get a sense of the valuation we could see in the near term. Remember, Bitcoin is down from those ~$120,000 highs not so long ago, so an entry point in the near term would see its average Bitcoin price fairly low. If the vote goes through on the 9th, I would expect Asset Entities to open a position fairly quickly.

Valuation

Valuation here is quite tricky to pinpoint since the company is going through such a transitional period. Asset Entities holds a market cap of roughly $102 million, with an enterprise value of just $100 million and no debt. On top of this, their current revenue stream is extremely small, just $344,000 in 1G25 and an annualized figure of $0.7 million. So at current levels, they look highly overvalued compared to that peer group sector. But keep in mind that the sector is formed of communication services companies, and if this merger goes through, Asset Entities will become a full Bitcoin treasury. As of now, on a trailing basis, they hold an EV to sales ratio of 131.06x and a price to sales ratio of 77x against sector medians of just 1.9x and 1.3x, respectively. Its price-to-book ratio of 38x also exceeds the peer group median of just 2x. What this all means is that the market isn't valuing Asset Entities as a small digital marketing business anymore. The probability of the merger going through has already been priced in by the market, and with the price of Bitcoin near all-time highs, Asset Entities finds itself quite overvalued. If the merger closes and the PIPE financing plus the Section 351 Bitcoin exchange go through, the combined entity’s balance sheet and growth story will reset entirely.

What's Next?

All eyes should be on the shareholder vote next week on the 9th of September. If you’ve held shares since before July 21st, then you’re eligible to vote. I expect it will pass, and we should see them open a Bitcoin position sometime after.
